title: ALCOA+ Principles for Data Integrity
text: Records generated during manufacturing and testing must be attributable, legible, contemporaneous, original and accurate, with the extended expectations that they also be complete, consistent, enduring and available. Each principle guards against a specific failure mode: attributability ties an action to a named person, contemporaneous recording prevents back-dating, and keeping the original or a verified true copy preserves the primary evidence. These expectations apply equally to paper and electronic systems and underpin every claim a site makes about the trustworthiness of its results.

title: Audit Trail Review for Electronic Records
text: Computerised systems that create or modify quality records must capture a secure, time-stamped log of who did what and when, including any changes to data and the reason for the change. Reviewers examine these logs as part of batch release, looking for unexplained modifications, deletions, repeated tests or changes made outside expected working patterns. The review frequency is risk-based: systems that directly determine product disposition are scrutinised every batch, while lower-impact systems are sampled periodically. Findings that suggest manipulation are escalated through the deviation process.

title: Electronic Records and Electronic Signatures
text: When a site chooses to keep records electronically instead of on paper, those records and any signatures applied to them must be as trustworthy and legally binding as their handwritten equivalents. Controls include limiting system access to authorised individuals, binding signatures to records so they cannot be copied or transferred, enforcing unique user credentials, and validating the software so it performs as intended. Open systems that transmit data across untrusted networks need additional encryption and integrity checks.

title: Cleaning Validation and Acceptance Limits
text: Shared product-contact equipment must be demonstrated to be cleaned effectively so that residue from one product, cleaning agents and microorganisms do not carry over into the next batch at unsafe levels. Acceptance limits are derived from health-based exposure data, typically a permitted daily exposure, and translated into a maximum allowable carryover for the surface area concerned. The worst-case product, hardest-to-clean location and most difficult residue are selected to challenge the procedure, and successful cleaning is confirmed across consecutive runs.

title: Swab and Rinse Sampling for Equipment Surfaces
text: After a cleaning procedure, residual contamination on equipment is measured by physically wiping a defined surface area with a moistened swab and by collecting a final rinse of the system. Swabs recover residue from hard-to-reach and worst-case locations, while rinse samples assess large or complex surfaces that cannot be swabbed directly. Recovered samples are tested for active substance, detergent and microbial load, and recovery studies establish how much of a known amount the sampling method actually retrieves so results can be corrected.

title: Equipment Qualification: Installation, Operational, Performance
text: Before a machine is used to make product it is shown to be fit for purpose in three documented stages. The first confirms it was delivered and installed according to specification, with utilities connected and drawings verified. The second challenges each function and control across its operating range, including alarms and safety interlocks. The third demonstrates that, running under realistic production conditions and often with actual materials, the equipment consistently produces output meeting predefined criteria. Each stage has a protocol approved before execution.

title: Process Validation Lifecycle
text: Confidence that a manufacturing process reliably yields conforming product is built across three connected phases. Process design captures knowledge from development and defines a control strategy. Process qualification confirms, at commercial scale, that the design performs as expected, usually through several consecutive successful batches monitored intensively. Continued verification then watches the process throughout routine production using trending and statistical signals, so drift is detected and the validated state is maintained rather than assumed from a one-time exercise.

title: Computerised System Validation
text: Software that manages quality-relevant data or controls equipment is verified to do what it is supposed to do, reliably and securely, before and during use. Effort is scaled to risk and to whether the system is configured off-the-shelf or custom built. Activities include defining user requirements, supplier assessment, specification and testing against those requirements, and controls over access, backups and change. Validation is treated as a lifecycle: any modification is assessed and, where needed, retested to preserve the qualified state.

title: Handling Manufacturing Deviations
text: Any departure from an approved instruction, specification or procedure is recorded, assessed for its effect on product quality, and investigated to find why it happened. Immediate actions contain the issue and protect affected batches, while a root-cause analysis distinguishes a one-off human slip from a systemic weakness. The impact assessment decides whether material can still be released. Deviations that recur or that indicate a deeper failure trigger corrective and preventive action so the same problem does not return.

title: Corrective and Preventive Action
text: When problems are found through deviations, complaints, audits or trends, the organisation acts both to fix the immediate cause and to stop the issue arising again elsewhere. A corrective action addresses an existing nonconformity; a preventive action removes the cause of a potential one before it occurs. Each action is justified by root-cause understanding, assigned an owner and due date, and checked afterwards for effectiveness, so closing the record genuinely reflects a resolved problem rather than a completed task.

title: Change Control Procedure
text: Proposed changes to facilities, equipment, materials, processes, methods or documents are evaluated and approved before implementation so that nothing alters the validated state without oversight. The proposal describes the change and its rationale; quality and technical reviewers assess risk, regulatory impact and what revalidation, retesting or documentation updates are required. Actions are tracked to completion and the change is verified as effective. Emergency changes follow an expedited route but still receive full retrospective review.

title: Out-of-Specification Result Investigation
text: When a laboratory test gives a result outside its acceptance criteria, the finding is investigated before it can be accepted or rejected. The first phase checks for an assignable laboratory cause such as a calculation error, equipment fault or analyst mistake, without discarding the original data. If no laboratory error is found, the investigation widens to manufacturing, considering process and sampling. Results are never simply retested away; any decision to invalidate the original value must be scientifically justified and documented.

title: Batch Record Review and Disposition
text: Before a batch is released for sale, an authorised person reviews the complete record of its manufacture and testing to confirm every step was performed and documented correctly. The review checks that critical parameters stayed within limits, materials were correct and reconciled, in-process and finished checks passed, and any deviations were closed with an acceptable conclusion. Only when the whole history supports the batch and all results meet specification is the product certified and made available for distribution.

title: Environmental Monitoring of Cleanrooms
text: Controlled production areas are routinely sampled to confirm that levels of airborne and surface contamination remain within limits appropriate to the activity. Monitoring covers viable organisms collected on growth media from the air, settle plates and contact plates, alongside non-viable airborne particle counts. Sampling locations and frequencies target the points of greatest risk to exposed product, and results are trended over time. Excursions beyond alert or action levels prompt investigation and, where warranted, corrective measures.

title: Cleanroom Classification and Air Cleanliness
text: Manufacturing rooms are graded by how few airborne particles they permit, with the cleanest grades reserved for operations where sterile product is exposed. Classification is established at rest and in operation by counting particles of defined sizes per volume of air. Cleaner zones are maintained at higher pressure than adjacent dirtier zones so air flows outward and contamination is kept out. Sufficient filtered air changes, controlled airflow patterns and qualified filters sustain the required grade during work.

title: HVAC System Qualification
text: The heating, ventilation and air-conditioning system that keeps production areas clean and stable is verified to deliver the required conditions. Qualification confirms filter integrity, air change rates, room pressure cascades, airflow direction, temperature and humidity, and recovery time after a disturbance. Because the air system is the primary defence against airborne contamination in graded areas, its performance is requalified periodically and after significant change, and key parameters such as differential pressure are monitored continuously during operation.

title: Water for Injection System Design
text: The highest grade of pharmaceutical water, used in products that enter the bloodstream, is produced by methods such as distillation or membrane processes that remove dissolved solids, organisms and bacterial endotoxin. The distribution loop is built from smooth, drainable stainless steel, kept continuously circulating and often held hot to discourage microbial growth, and designed without stagnant dead legs where contamination could settle. Sanitisation and continuous monitoring of conductivity and microbial quality keep the system fit for sterile use.

title: Purified Water System Monitoring
text: A purified water system is sampled on a defined schedule to confirm it continues to meet chemical and microbiological quality. Online instruments track conductivity and total organic carbon, while periodic samples from use points and the loop are tested for microbial counts. A rotating plan ensures every outlet is checked over time. Alert and action limits are set below the formal specification so that an adverse trend is caught and corrected before water that fails quality is ever used in production.

title: Aseptic Processing and Contamination Control
text: When a product cannot be sterilised in its final container, its components are sterilised separately and then combined and filled under conditions that exclude contamination. Success depends on a contamination control strategy spanning facility design, air quality, personnel behaviour, sterilised components and validated filling. Because there is no terminal step to correct lapses, every intervention is minimised and controlled, operators are highly trained, and the whole approach is challenged by simulating the process with growth medium in place of product.

title: Aseptic Process Simulation
text: The ability of an aseptic filling operation to keep product sterile is demonstrated by running the entire process with a sterile growth medium substituted for the real product. Filled units are incubated and examined for microbial growth; the acceptance criterion is essentially zero contaminated units across a representative number filled. The simulation deliberately includes the routine and worst-case interventions operators would normally perform, and is repeated periodically and after relevant changes to confirm the line and its staff remain in control.

title: Moist Heat Sterilisation by Autoclave
text: Heat-stable items and certain products are rendered sterile using pressurised saturated steam, which transfers energy efficiently to destroy microorganisms. The cycle is defined by temperature, pressure and exposure time, and its lethality is expressed as an accumulated measure of microbial kill. Validation maps the chamber to find the slowest-to-heat location, places biological indicators there, and proves the target reduction in organisms is achieved throughout the load. Each routine cycle is recorded and reviewed against the qualified parameters.

title: Dry Heat Depyrogenation of Glassware
text: Glass containers destined for injectable products are passed through sustained high dry heat that both sterilises them and destroys bacterial endotoxin left behind by dead organisms. Because endotoxin causes fever even when no living microbe remains, the process targets a large reduction in endotoxin challenge rather than microbial kill alone. The tunnel or oven is mapped for temperature uniformity, and endotoxin-spiked indicators confirm the required reduction is reached at the coolest point of the load during the exposure time.

title: Visual Inspection of Injectable Products
text: Filled injectable units are examined to detect and remove any that contain visible foreign particles or that show container or closure defects. Inspection may be performed by trained operators against controlled lighting and backgrounds or by automated vision systems, with operators qualified against sets containing known good and defective units. Detection of small particles depends on contrast, magnification and inspection time. Defect rates are trended, and clusters of a particular defect prompt investigation of the upstream cause.

title: Instrument Calibration Programme
text: Measuring instruments that affect product quality are checked against traceable references at defined intervals to confirm they read accurately within stated tolerances. Each instrument has a unique identifier, an assigned interval based on its importance and stability, and a status label showing when it is next due. An instrument found outside tolerance triggers an assessment of the results it produced since its last good calibration, because those measurements may have been wrong and the affected product may need review.

title: Preventive Maintenance of Production Equipment
text: Equipment is serviced on a planned schedule to keep it running reliably and within its qualified condition, rather than waiting for it to fail during production. Maintenance tasks, frequencies and responsible roles are defined for each asset, and completed work is recorded. Activities that could affect a qualified or validated state are assessed beforehand, and significant repairs or part replacements may require requalification. A well-run programme reduces unplanned downtime and the deviations that breakdowns cause.

title: Stability Testing and Shelf-Life
text: How long a product keeps its quality, and the storage conditions it needs, are established by storing batches under controlled temperature and humidity and testing them at intervals. Long-term conditions support the labelled expiry, while elevated conditions probe how the product degrades and give early warning of problems. Selected attributes such as potency, related substances and physical appearance are trended over time, and at least one batch a year is usually placed on an ongoing programme to confirm the shelf-life holds.

title: Analytical Method Validation
text: Before a test method is used to make quality decisions, it is shown to be suitable for its purpose by demonstrating characteristics appropriate to the test. These typically include specificity to distinguish the analyte from interferences, accuracy against a known value, precision on repeated measurement, linearity across a range, and the limits at which a substance can be detected or reliably quantified. Robustness checks how small deliberate changes in conditions affect results, giving confidence the method performs in routine use.

title: Supplier and Vendor Qualification
text: Organisations that provide materials, components or services affecting product quality are assessed and approved before use and monitored thereafter. Qualification considers the criticality of what is supplied and may involve questionnaires, document review, sample testing and on-site audit. Approved suppliers are listed, and ongoing performance such as quality of deliveries, complaints and audit outcomes determines whether approval continues. A risk-based approach focuses the most effort on suppliers of the most critical materials.

title: Incoming Material Testing and Release
text: Materials received for use in manufacturing are quarantined on arrival and not used until they have been sampled, tested against their specification and released by quality. Identity is confirmed for every received container, while other attributes may be tested on a representative sample. Certificates from approved suppliers may reduce testing once a reliable history is established, but identity verification remains. Only material meeting specification is released; the rest is rejected and segregated to prevent accidental use.

title: Quality Risk Management
text: Decisions about product quality are guided by a structured assessment of risk rather than by habit alone. The approach identifies what could go wrong, estimates how likely it is and how severe its effect would be, and how readily it would be detected, then decides which risks to reduce, accept or monitor. The rigour of the assessment is matched to the importance of the decision. Tools range from simple ranking to detailed failure-mode analysis, and conclusions are revisited as new knowledge emerges.

title: Annual Product Quality Review
text: Each product is reviewed at least yearly to confirm the consistency of its process and the appropriateness of its specifications, and to spot trends needing action. The review draws together batch results, deviations, changes, complaints, returns, stability data and any rejected material across the period. Patterns that emerge only across many batches, such as a slow drift in a parameter, become visible at this scale and feed improvement actions, even when every individual batch met its limits.

title: GMP Training and Personnel Qualification
text: Everyone whose work affects product quality is trained for their role and kept current as procedures change. Training covers the principles of good manufacturing practice plus the specific tasks a person performs, and effectiveness is checked rather than assumed, for example by assessment or observed performance. Records show who was trained on what and when, so that only qualified people carry out a given operation. People are retrained when procedures are revised or when performance indicates a gap.

title: Cleanroom Gowning and Aseptic Behaviour
text: People are the largest source of contamination in clean areas, so operators entering graded zones follow a defined sequence to cover skin and hair with sterilised garments and move in slow, deliberate ways that avoid disturbing airflow over exposed product. Entry into the cleanest areas requires a qualifying gowning assessment in which the gowned operator is sampled for organisms. Behaviours such as minimising interventions, not reaching over open product and controlling movement are reinforced through training and observation.

title: Controlled Document and Procedure Management
text: Procedures, specifications and other quality documents are managed so that only the current, approved version is available for use and obsolete copies are withdrawn. Each document is uniquely identified and version controlled, reviewed and approved by authorised roles before issue, and periodically reviewed to stay accurate. A change to a document follows a defined revision route. Controlling documents this way ensures staff always work to correct instructions and provides a traceable history of what was in force at any time.

title: Label Control and Reconciliation
text: Printed packaging materials that carry product identity are tightly controlled to prevent a mix-up that could put the wrong information on a product. Labels are stored securely, issued against a specific batch in a counted quantity, and the numbers issued, used, damaged and returned are reconciled at the end of packaging. A discrepancy beyond an allowed margin is investigated before the batch proceeds, because an unexplained shortfall could mean labels reached the wrong product.

title: Product Complaint Handling
text: Reports from patients, customers or healthcare providers about a possible quality problem are recorded, assessed for seriousness and investigated to determine whether the product is genuinely affected and why. The assessment considers whether other batches could share the issue and whether the problem poses a risk to health, which may prompt wider action up to a recall. The complainant receives a response, trends across complaints are watched for systemic issues, and findings feed corrective and preventive action.

title: Product Recall Procedure
text: When a marketed batch is found to present a quality or safety problem, a defined process retrieves it from the distribution chain quickly and completely. Distribution records identify where affected product went so customers can be notified to the appropriate level, and regulators are informed as required. Recovered stock is segregated and accounted for against what was distributed to judge how effective the recall was. The process is tested periodically so it works under the time pressure a real event brings.

title: Quality Agreements with Contract Manufacturers
text: When manufacturing or testing is outsourced, a written agreement defines which party is responsible for each quality activity so nothing falls through the gap between organisations. It covers who controls changes and deviations, who reviews and releases batches, how complaints and recalls are handled, and the right of the contract giver to audit the contract acceptor. A clear agreement, backed by oversight of the partner's performance, keeps accountability for product quality with the company that places the product on the market.

title: Backup and Archival of GxP Data
text: Electronic records that support quality decisions are protected against loss and kept retrievable for as long as they are required. Regular backups are taken and periodically restored to prove they actually work, copies are held securely and separately from the live system, and archived data remains readable as software evolves. Retention periods reflect regulatory requirements and product life. Because a lost or unreadable record undermines the evidence behind a released batch, backup and archival are treated as integrity controls, not just IT housekeeping.

title: Temperature Mapping of Storage Areas
text: Warehouses, cold rooms and other spaces that store temperature-sensitive product are studied to confirm the whole volume stays within the required range, not just where a single gauge happens to sit. Calibrated sensors are distributed throughout, including likely hot and cold spots near doors, walls and air outlets, and readings are logged across representative conditions including a power interruption. The results justify where permanent monitoring probes are placed and set the alarm limits that protect stored material thereafter.
